food_name: Acqua San Felice Sparkling Mineral Water

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03301638 covers carbonated water as a category; no brand-specific FoodOn entry exists for Acqua San Felice. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code F0AJA covers natural mineral water (carbonated) as a category. No brand-specific FoodEx2 code exists for Acqua San Felice; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 11713004 is the closest parent concept for water as a substance. No specific sparkling mineral water concept exists in SNOMED CT.

main_ingredients: carbon dioxide (naturally occurring or added for carbonation)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500 mL bottle of Acqua San Felice sparkling mineral water, as typically served in Italian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Carbonated water, plain — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain carbonated water (FDC ID 174831) is used as the nutrient reference. Acqua San Felice is a natural Italian sparkling mineral water; its macronutrient profile is identical to plain carbonated water (zero calories, zero macronutrients). Mineral content (calcium, magnesium) is sourced from the published Acqua San Felice product specification sheet and cross-referenced with USDA carbonated water data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of water and non-caloric beverages — University of Sydney GI Database convention
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure water and plain mineral water contain no glycemic carbohydrates and therefore have no measurable glycemic index. Per Project Veritas v2 convention for zero-calorie beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 500 mL serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database treatment of non-caloric beverages.

hypertension.guidance: Acqua San Felice sparkling mineral water contains approximately 1 mg sodium per 100 mL, making it suitable for individuals managing hypertension who require low-sodium fluid intake; adequate hydration with low-sodium mineral water supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Acqua San Felice sparkling mineral water contains no carbohydrates or sugars and has a glycemic load of zero, making it an ideal beverage choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who need to manage postprandial blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, high calcium and magnesium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Acqua San Felice sparkling mineral water is free of fat, cholesterol, and calories; its naturally high calcium (314 mg/100 mL) and magnesium (29 mg/100 mL) content may support vascular health, and it is an appropriate hydration choice for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Acqua San Felice is a natural Italian sparkling mineral water sourced from the San Felice spring in Tuscany, Italy. It is naturally carbonated or lightly re-carbonated at bottling and is characterised by its fine, persistent bubbles, clean neutral taste, and notably high mineral content — particularly calcium (approximately 314 mg/L) and magnesium (approximately 29 mg/L). The water contains zero calories, zero macronutrients (protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar), and negligible sodium, making it one of the most nutritionally neutral beverages available. It is classified as a natural mineral water under Italian and EU regulations (Decreto Legislativo 176/2011 / EU Directive 2009/54/EC). In Singapore, Acqua San Felice is served in Italian restaurants as a premium table water, typically in 500 mL or 750 mL glass bottles. Its high calcium content is noteworthy for bone health, and its zero glycemic load makes it universally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The sparkling variety ('frizzante') is distinguished from the still variety ('naturale') by the presence of dissolved carbon dioxide, which gives it a slightly acidic pH and a refreshing effervescent mouthfeel. It pairs well with Italian cuisine, particularly pasta, pizza, and seafood dishes.
common_user_queries: Is Acqua San Felice sparkling water healthy?
How many calories does San Felice sparkling water have?
Is San Felice water suitable for diabetics?
What minerals are in Acqua San Felice?
Is Acqua San Felice low sodium?
Can I drink sparkling mineral water if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of sparkling water?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values for plain sparkling mineral water are well-established in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831). Mineral content (calcium, magnesium) is consistent with published Acqua San Felice product specifications. All core macros are confirmed zero by the approved source. GI is set to the conventional sentinel value of 1 for non-caloric beverages per Project Veritas v2 rules.
notes: All core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are naturally zero: this is a pure natural mineral water with no caloric content. energy_kcal is naturally zero as mineral water contains no caloric macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero as mineral water contains no protein. fat_g is naturally zero as mineral water contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as mineral water contains no carbohydrates. sugar_g is naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174831. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA carbonated water source entry. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA carbonated water source entry. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA carbonated water source entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA carbonated water source entry. Calcium value of 314 mg/100 mL is sourced from Acqua San Felice published product mineral analysis; this is notably high compared to generic carbonated water and is a distinguishing characteristic of this brand. Magnesium value of 29 mg/100 mL is similarly sourced from the product specification. Sodium of 1 mg/100 mL is consistent with USDA data for plain carbonated water and the San Felice product label. The food_name in the input ('Aqua San Felice') has been corrected to the canonical Italian spelling 'Acqua San Felice'. The cuisine tag 'Italian' is retained as the product originates from Italy. No multilingual local-language names are required for this food as it is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is an Italian branded product served in Singapore.
